title: Add unit tests for core/circuit_breaker.py
body:
## Description
The `core/circuit_breaker.py` module implements the circuit breaker pattern that prevents cascading LLM failures, but it has no dedicated test coverage. New contributors can write focused unit tests to verify its behaviour.

## Tasks
- [ ] Write a test that verifies the breaker opens after 5 consecutive failures
- [ ] Write a test that verifies the breaker auto-recovers after 60 seconds
- [ ] Write a test that verifies calls are rejected while the breaker is open
- [ ] Write a test that verifies the failure counter resets after a successful call

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests live in `tests/test_circuit_breaker.py`
- All tests pass with `pytest tests/test_circuit_breaker.py -v`
- Uses existing test patterns from `tests/conftest.py`

## Good First Issue Notes
This is a great starting point — the circuit breaker logic is self-contained and easy to unit test with mocking.

title: Add type hints to data/pm_kisan_rules.py
body:
## Description
The `data/pm_kisan_rules.py` file contains the hardcoded PM-KISAN eligibility rules but lacks Python type annotations. Adding type hints improves IDE support, catches bugs early, and aligns with the rest of the codebase where `core/` files already use type hints.

## Tasks
- [ ] Add parameter and return-type annotations to all functions in `data/pm_kisan_rules.py`
- [ ] Follow the typing conventions used in `core/fact_agent.py` and `core/schema.py`
- [ ] Run `mypy data/pm_kisan_rules.py` (or `pyright`) and resolve any errors

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every function has complete type annotations
- No mypy / pyright type errors introduced
- Existing functionality is unchanged (all tests still pass)

## Good First Issue Notes
A great beginner task — no logic changes needed, just reading existing code and adding annotations.

title: Add GET /schemes endpoint listing supported government schemes
body:
## Description
The UFAC Engine currently only supports PM-KISAN, but the architecture is designed to scale to 150+ schemes. A new `GET /schemes` endpoint would give API consumers a way to discover which schemes are currently supported without reading the source code.

## Proposed Response
```json
{
  "schemes": [
    {
      "id": "pm_kisan",
      "name": "PM-KISAN",
      "description": "Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi – direct income support to farmer families",
      "supported": true
    }
  ],
  "total": 1
}
```

## Tasks
- [ ] Add a `SUPPORTED_SCHEMES` constant in `core/constants.py`
- [ ] Add `GET /schemes` route in `api/app.py`
- [ ] Write a test in `tests/test_api.py`
- [ ] Document the endpoint in README.md under **API Endpoints**

## Acceptance Criteria
- `GET /schemes` returns HTTP 200 with the scheme list
- Endpoint is visible in Swagger UI (`/docs`)
- At least one passing test

## Good First Issue Notes
A simple read-only endpoint with no external dependencies — perfect for a first contribution.

title: Pin all dependency versions in requirements.txt
body:
## Description
Several packages in `requirements.txt` are listed without pinned versions. Unpinned dependencies can cause silent breakage when a new package version is released. All versions should be pinned so that `pip install -r requirements.txt` produces an identical environment every time.

## Tasks
- [ ] Run `pip freeze` in a clean virtual environment after installing the current `requirements.txt`
- [ ] Update each package entry to use `==` with the resolved version
- [ ] Verify the server still starts after the change
- [ ] Add a comment at the top of `requirements.txt` explaining how to update versions

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every line in `requirements.txt` uses `package==X.Y.Z` format
- `pip install -r requirements.txt` succeeds in a fresh virtual environment
- All existing tests still pass

## Good First Issue Notes
No code logic involved — just dependency management. Great for learning about Python packaging.

title: Add input validation for negative and out-of-range numeric values in /check
body:
## Description
The `POST /check` endpoint accepts user data but does not reject clearly invalid numeric inputs such as negative land area or negative annual income. An attacker or misconfigured client could send `"annual_income": -99999` and receive a confusing assessment result.

## Tasks
- [ ] Add Pydantic validators to `core/schema.py` for all numeric fields (`annual_income`, `land_area`, etc.) to reject negative values
- [ ] Return a clear HTTP 422 with a descriptive message for invalid inputs
- [ ] Add tests in `tests/test_api.py` for negative / out-of-range inputs

## Acceptance Criteria
- `POST /check` with `"annual_income": -1` returns HTTP 422
- Error message clearly explains the constraint
- Existing valid requests are unaffected

## Good First Issue Notes
Uses Pydantic validators — great way to learn FastAPI input validation.

title: Add CHANGELOG.md to track version history
body:
## Description
The project is at v2.0.0 but has no `CHANGELOG.md` documenting what changed between versions. A changelog helps contributors understand the project's evolution and helps users know what to expect when upgrading.

## Tasks
- [ ] Create `CHANGELOG.md` at the project root following the [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.0.0/) format
- [ ] Document the changes introduced in **v2.0.0** (security hardening, circuit breaker, caching, PostgreSQL support)
- [ ] Document the baseline **v1.0.0** features (5-agent architecture, RAG pipeline, PM-KISAN rules)
- [ ] Add a link to `CHANGELOG.md` from `README.md`

## Acceptance Criteria
- `CHANGELOG.md` exists at project root
- Entries follow Keep a Changelog format (Added, Changed, Fixed, Removed sections)
- README.md links to it

## Good First Issue Notes
Documentation-only change — no code required. Great for learning the project's history.

title: Move hardcoded strings in api/app.py to core/constants.py
body:
## Description
`api/app.py` contains several hardcoded string literals (security header names/values, error message strings, endpoint descriptions) that are used directly inline. These should be moved to `core/constants.py` to make them easy to find and update, consistent with the project's existing pattern of centralising magic values.

## Tasks
- [ ] Identify all hardcoded string literals in `api/app.py` (e.g. header names like `"X-Content-Type-Options"`, response phrases)
- [ ] Add appropriately named constants in `core/constants.py` (e.g. `HEADER_CONTENT_TYPE_OPTIONS = "X-Content-Type-Options"`)
- [ ] Replace inline strings in `api/app.py` with the new constants
- [ ] Verify all existing tests still pass

## Acceptance Criteria
- No raw security-header-name strings remain in `api/app.py`
- New constants are grouped under a clear comment in `core/constants.py`
- All tests pass

## Good First Issue Notes
A pure refactoring task — no new logic. Helps you explore both the API layer and the constants module.

title: Add shared pytest fixtures for common test data in tests/conftest.py
body:
## Description
The test files (`test_api.py`, `test_cache.py`, `test_error_handling.py`) each define their own sample user-data dictionaries. This duplication means that if the data model changes, tests break in multiple places. Shared fixtures in `tests/conftest.py` would solve this.

## Tasks
- [ ] Add a `sample_eligible_user` fixture to `tests/conftest.py` returning a dict representing a clearly eligible PM-KISAN applicant
- [ ] Add a `sample_ineligible_user` fixture returning a dict representing an ineligible applicant
- [ ] Add a `sample_incomplete_user` fixture with missing required fields
- [ ] Refactor existing test files to use these shared fixtures instead of inline dicts

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fixtures are defined in `tests/conftest.py`
- All existing tests still pass after refactoring
- No duplicate sample-data dicts remain in individual test files

## Good First Issue Notes
A great refactoring task that improves test maintainability without changing any application logic.

title: Add confidence score colour coding to the React UI
body:
## Description
The frontend (`UI/`) displays the confidence score as a plain number. A simple colour-coded indicator (green for high confidence ≥ 80, amber for medium 65–79, red for low < 65) would make the result much easier to interpret at a glance.

## Tasks
- [ ] Locate the confidence score display component in `UI/components/`
- [ ] Add a helper function `getConfidenceColour(score: number)` that returns a Tailwind CSS colour class based on the thresholds defined in `core/constants.py` (CONFIDENCE_HIGH = 80, CONFIDENCE_MEDIUM = 65)
- [ ] Apply the colour class to the score badge/text
- [ ] Test visually with scores in the high, medium, and low ranges using the demo button

## Acceptance Criteria
- Score ≥ 80 appears green
- Score 65–79 appears amber/yellow
- Score < 65 appears red
- All thresholds match the backend constants (80 / 65)

## Good First Issue Notes
A self-contained UI change — no backend knowledge required. Great for frontend contributors.

title: Add /metrics and /cache-stats endpoint tests to tests/test_api.py
body:
## Description
The `GET /metrics` and `POST /metrics-reset` endpoints are used in production for monitoring but are not covered by any tests in `tests/test_api.py`. Adding tests for these endpoints ensures that monitoring data is always returned in the correct shape and that the reset operation works as expected.

## Tasks
- [ ] Add a test that calls `GET /metrics` and verifies the response contains expected top-level keys (`total_requests`, `cache_hits`, `average_latency_ms`, etc.)
- [ ] Add a test that calls `POST /metrics-reset` and then `GET /metrics` to confirm counters are zeroed
- [ ] Add a test that calls `GET /cache-stats` and verifies its response shape
- [ ] Use the existing `TestClient` setup already in `tests/test_api.py`

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least 3 new tests added
- All tests pass with `pytest tests/test_api.py -v`
- Tests cover both the happy path and the reset flow

## Good First Issue Notes
Uses the existing FastAPI `TestClient` — no mocking required. Great for learning API testing patterns.
